summary: Gruber highlights investigative reporting by Johnny Lin exposing scam apps in the App Store's top-grossing lists, particularly fake virus scanners charging $100/week subscriptions. He argues that anti-virus apps have no place on iOS since sandboxing makes them useless, and their presence falsely implies Apple thinks users need such software. The scam pattern exploits App Store search ads to drive downloads, then tricks a small percentage into expensive subscriptions. Gruber calls out the irony that legitimate developers face capricious rejections while obvious scams reach the top 10 grossing list. He demands Apple remove these apps, reform search ad policies, police top-grossing lists, and refund every dollar to victims.
key_points: iOS sandboxing makes anti-virus apps functionally useless, so their mere presence in the App Store creates a false impression that users need them
Scam apps exploit App Store search ads to buy top placement for common keywords, then spring expensive subscription prompts on unsuspecting users
The App Store review process fails at its core purpose when it blocks legitimate developers while letting obvious scams reach the top 10 grossing list
Apple bears responsibility for not flagging these apps after they started generating significant revenue from clearly predatory subscription pricing
Apple should proactively police top-grossing lists since the most important scams to catch are the successful ones
money_quotes: There should be no "virus and malware" scanners in the App Store. None. iOS does not need anti-virus software.
By allowing them to be listed on the store, it creates the false impression that Apple thinks you might need anti-virus software.
But do-nothing anti-virus utilities that are scamming people into $100/week subscriptions? That's downright criminal.

Given how many legitimate developers are still having problems getting their apps approved due to seemingly capricious App Store reviewer decisions, it's doubly outrageous that these apps have made their way onto the store in the first place.
These are the exact sort of apps that the App Store review process should be primarily looking to block.
It's downright mind boggling that this horrendous "Mobile protection :Clean & Security VPN" app made it all the way into the top 10 without getting flagged.
The most important scams to catch are the successful ones.
Every single dollar these apps have generated should be refunded to the victims of these scams.
key_insight: The App Store review process is fundamentally misallocating its enforcement, blocking legitimate developers over minor issues while letting obvious subscription scams reach the top-grossing charts unchecked.
